literal: a vulgar term for the male member
natural: the most severe, most grammatically productive root in the mat system — the base for dozens of derived words covered elsewhere in this pack
whoUses: extremely widespread across informal/vulgar registers regardless of age, though usage in front of elders, strangers, or in professional settings is a genuine breach of norms
usageContext: Rarely said in complete isolation — its real linguistic weight comes from how productively it combines with prefixes and suffixes (see this pack's grammar file). On its own, it functions as a severe standalone exclamation or the base of the even more common phrase 'на хуй'.
warningNotes: One of the most severe words in Russian by any native speaker's judgment — legally restricted from broadcast media and public signage. Recognition only; there is no learner context where producing this is a good idea.

examples.text: (в разговоре, для распознавания) Да ну на хуй.
examples.translation: (for recognition only) 'Screw this' / dismissive exclamation of disgust.

literal: literally 'onto/to [the root]', functioning as a directional intensifier
natural: 'get lost' / 'to hell with [it]' — a dismissive, hostile intensifying phrase
whoUses: genuine anger or dismissal in very informal/vulgar registers
usageContext: One of the most common mat-based dismissal phrases — tells someone or something to go away in the harshest possible register. Also appears attached to a request as an intensifier ('зачем нахуй', roughly 'why the hell').
warningNotes: Reads as genuinely aggressive in real conversation, not just casually blunt — do not produce this even in jest with people you don't know extremely well.

examples.translation: (for recognition only) A severe dismissive curse, roughly 'get the hell away from me'.

literal: root + по- prefix, literally something like 'along/according to [the root]'
whoUses: very informal speech among peers, all ages
usageContext: An extremely common way to express total indifference — much more casual-sounding to native ears than its literal roughness might suggest to a learner, but still solidly taboo register, not appropriate outside informal peer contexts.

examples.translation: (for recognition only) 'I couldn't care less about that.'

whoUses: widespread informal use, all ages, especially among peers
usageContext: One of the most flexible mat-derived nouns — can describe a broken object, a bad situation, a poorly made thing, or nonsense someone is saying. Its frequency and semantic breadth make it feel milder in daily use than its root would suggest, but it remains taboo register.

examples.text: (для распознавания) Это какая-то хуйня, а не план.
examples.romaji: Éto kakáya-ta khuynyá, a nye plan.
examples.translation: (for recognition only) 'This isn't a plan, it's garbage.'

literal: adverb/predicate form of the adjective derived from the root
whoUses: widespread informal use describing a bad state of affairs, feeling ill, or a situation gone wrong
usageContext: Functions as both an adverb and an impersonal predicate of state ('мне хуёво' — 'I feel terrible'), following the same neuter-short-form-as-adverb pattern as plokho ('badly') from плохой, just built on a taboo root.

literal: root intensified with о-/у- and the adjectival/adverbial suffix -енн-
natural: 'incredibly / awesomely' — often used approvingly, unlike most of this word family
whoUses: informal peer speech, especially younger speakers, in both positive and negative exclamations
usageContext: A useful reminder that severity and negativity are separate axes in mat — this word is very frequently used to praise something ('охуенно!', roughly 'that's awesome!'), yet it remains taboo/vulgar register regardless of the positive sentiment.

examples.translation: (for recognition only) 'That was awesome!' — positive in meaning, still vulgar register.

literal: a vulgar term for the female genitalia
natural: the second core root of the system — as a standalone exclamation, signals something has gone severely, irreversibly wrong
whoUses: widespread across vulgar registers; using it toward or about a person, rather than as an abstract exclamation, escalates it further
usageContext: Alongside the root covered in matprof.khuy, this is the other principal root of the system, generating its own family of derived nouns/adjectives (see пиздец, пиздато in this same file).
warningNotes: As severe as Russian gets — legally restricted from broadcast contexts, and not something a learner should ever produce, even as an exclamation alone.

examples.text: (для распознавания) Всё, пизда планам на выходные.
examples.translation: (for recognition only) 'Well, that's the end of our weekend plans.'

natural: 'that's it' / 'disaster' / 'we're done for' — an extremely common all-purpose exclamation of disaster or extreme intensity
whoUses: very widespread informal use across ages, despite its severity
usageContext: Functions as a standalone exclamation ('пиздец!', roughly 'oh no' / 'that's insane') or as a predicate ('это пиздец' — 'this is a disaster'). Its sheer frequency in casual speech doesn't reduce how taboo it's judged to be in formal contexts.
warningNotes: Extremely frequent in casual native speech, which can mislead learners into thinking it's mild — it is still squarely taboo register and inappropriate in professional or mixed-generation company.

examples.text: (для распознавания) Пиздец, мы опоздали на самолёт.
examples.translation: (for recognition only) 'Damn it, we missed our flight.'

literal: adverb/predicate form of an adjective derived from the same root as пизда
natural: 'awesome' / 'great' — a positive slang intensifier, parallel to охуенно
whoUses: informal peer speech, especially younger and internet-influenced speakers
usageContext: Another example of a mat-derived word used approvingly rather than as an insult — describes something impressively good, with the same taboo-register caveat as охуенно.

literal: a vulgar verb for the sex act
natural: the third core root — the base verb behind a large family of prefixed derivatives
whoUses: widespread across vulgar registers; also functions as a standalone shocked exclamation
usageContext: As a bare infinitive exclamation ('ебать!'), expresses shock or strong reaction to something unexpected. Its real productivity, as covered in this pack's grammar file, comes from prefixed forms like наебать ('to con someone') and заебать ('to exhaust someone's patience').
warningNotes: One of the core severe roots — recognition only, never appropriate for a learner to produce, including as a 'harmless' exclamation.

examples.text: (для распознавания) Ебать, я не ожидал такого поворота.
examples.romaji: Yebát', ya nye azhidál takóva pavaróta.
examples.translation: (for recognition only) A shocked exclamation at an unexpected turn of events.

literal: the root verb + the completive prefix на-
whoUses: informal speech describing being scammed or cheated, all ages who use vulgar register at all
usageContext: A concrete, frequently-heard example of the prefixation system this pack's grammar file describes — the на- prefix here contributes the same completive 'do it fully to someone' sense it would on a neutral verb.

examples.text: (для распознавания) Он меня наебал с деньгами.
examples.translation: (for recognition only) 'He conned me out of money.'

literal: the root verb + the outward-directional prefix вы- + reflexive -ся
natural: 'to show off / act arrogant / put on airs'
whoUses: informal peer speech, often used critically about someone else's behavior
usageContext: Always directed at a third party's behavior you're criticizing as arrogant or showy — another clear example of the productive prefix system, paralleling how выделяться ('to stand out') is built the same way from a neutral root.

examples.translation: (for recognition only) 'Stop showing off in front of everyone.'

literal: a vulgar term historically meaning 'promiscuous woman/prostitute'
natural: as an exclamation, roughly 'damn it' / 'shit' — also functions as a near-meaningless discourse filler for heavy mat-users (see this pack's grammar file)
whoUses: extremely widespread as an exclamation and filler in informal/vulgar registers, though directing it at a specific woman as a literal insult is a distinct, more hostile use
usageContext: One of the most frequent mat words in casual native speech, largely because of its filler/discourse-particle function — many uses carry little to no literal meaning and are closer to a verbal tic than an insult. Still solidly taboo register in any formal or mixed-generation context.
warningNotes: Its sheer frequency in overheard casual speech can make it feel unremarkable — it remains genuinely taboo register and is not appropriate for a learner to produce, especially not directed at a person.

examples.translation: (for recognition only, filler use) 'Damn it, the bus left.'

literal: literally 'female dog', used as a vulgar insult ('bitch')
natural: a hostile insult toward a person, or a standalone intensifying exclamation of frustration
whoUses: widespread as both a direct insult and a filler-like intensifier in informal/vulgar registers
usageContext: Not one of the four core mat roots in the strict linguistic sense, but behaves the same way functionally — it's ubiquitous as a stand-alone exclamation of anger ('сука!') and frequently paired with other mat words for emphasis ('вот сука' etc.), which is why it belongs alongside this word family for a learner's purposes.
warningNotes: Directed at a specific person, this is a genuine, hostile insult — even as a general exclamation it is solidly taboo register, not casual venting a learner should imitate.

examples.translation: (for recognition only, exclamation use) 'Damn it, it broke again.'
